did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically multiple convolutional neural networks (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, MobileNet-V2, Xception, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2, and DenseNet201) trained on the HAM10000 dataset, combined via a collective intelligence-based decision fusion module to classify skin lesions including melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel skin lesion classification system based on deep learning and collective intelligence. It details the methodology involving multiple convolutional neural networks (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, MobileNet-V2, Xception, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2, and DenseNet201) trained on the HAM10000 dataset, describes the implementation of a decision fusion module, and reports specific experimental results including validation accuracy and confusion matrices.

title: Skin Lesion Classification Using Collective Intelligence of Multiple Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin lesion classification system utilizing the collective intelligence of multiple convolutional neural networks to assist in the early detection of malignant lesions, including melanoma. The researchers trained nine individual CNNs (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, MobileNet-V2, Xception, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2, DenseNet201, and GoogLeNet-Places365) on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven diagnostic classes. To combine these models, the authors developed a decision fusion module that calculates weights for each network based on their individual performance to produce a final collective prediction. The system was evaluated by comparing its validation accuracy against the best-performing individual network. The key result demonstrated that the proposed collective intelligence-based system achieved a validation accuracy approximately 3 percent higher than the best individual network, confirming its superior performance.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic classes of skin lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a skin lesion classification system using a collective intelligence-based decision fusion module to predict one of seven discrete diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 dataset. The task is explicitly defined as classifying skin lesions, including melanoma, into specific classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large public dataset of dermatoscopic images introduced in the ISIC 2018 challenge, containing 10,015 images split into two parts (HAM10000_images_part1.zip with 5000 images and HAM10000_images_part2.zip with 5015 images). It covers seven diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions.
classes_used: akiec (Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease)

use.training: Used for training the individual CNNs (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, MobileNet-V2, Xception, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2, DenseNet201) and the GoogLeNet-Places365 variant. Data augmentation was applied to balance classes, specifically adding 200 images to underrepresented classes and removing corresponding images from the nevus class.
use.validation: Used for individual validation to select the best-performing neural networks based on accuracy (threshold of 75%).
use.testing: Used to establish the weight matrix for the collective intelligence system and to evaluate the final performance of the proposed Collective Intelligence System (CIS) and individual networks.
use.other: The dataset was split into two parts; the first part (70% used for training, 30% for validation) and the second part (used for testing/weight matrix establishment).

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale visual recognition dataset used for pretraining some of the convolutional neural networks.

use.training: Used for pretraining GoogLeNet and other networks before fine-tuning on HAM10000.

datasets_used.name: Places-365
datasets_used.description: A large-scale scene recognition dataset used for pretraining one of the neural networks.

use.training: Used for pretraining GoogLeNet-Places365 before fine-tuning on HAM10000.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using validation accuracy and confusion matrices, with a qualitative comparison stating the proposed system was 'about 3 percent better' than the best individual network.

Collective Intelligence System (CIS).Validation Accuracy: Approximately 3 percent better than the best performing individual network (ResNet-50 or DenseNet201)

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that the proposed Collective Intelligence System (CIS) achieved a validation accuracy approximately 3 percent better than the best-performing individual network. Individual network performances were used to establish a weight matrix for the ensemble. The best individual networks (ResNet-50 and DenseNet201) showed high validation accuracy (93.5%). The table in the text contains some apparent OCR errors or formatting issues for ResNet-101 and InceptionResNet-V2 validation accuracies (listed as 8 and 50.2 respectively), but the CIS improvement is explicitly stated as ~3% over the best individual.

architecture_used.main_model: Collective Intelligence-based System (CIS) utilizing an ensemble of nine Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): AlexNet, GoogLeNet, GoogLeNet-Places365, MobileNet-V2, Xception, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2, and DenseNet201.
architecture_used.pretraining: AlexNet, GoogLeNet, MobileNet-V2, Xception, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2, and DenseNet201 were pretrained on the ImageNet dataset. GoogLeNet-Places365 was pretrained on the Places-365 dataset. Models were downloaded from the Matlab 'File Exchange' source.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the provided text.

architecture_details.fusion_module: Collective Decision Block (CDB) that calculates a weight matrix [W] based on individual network performance on a testing set and combines weighted decisions.
architecture_details.weight_calculation: Weights (Wi,j) calculated as the ratio of correctly classified images for class Cj by network NNi to the total number of images for class Cj in the testing set.
architecture_details.decision_logic: Weighted decision matrix [D] computed by multiplying weights [W] by individual binary decisions. Final class Cm is selected by maximizing the sum of weighted decisions across networks for each class.
architecture_details.implementation_tool: Matlab and its Deep Learning Toolbox.

network_specifics.AlexNet: 8 layers (5 convolutional, 3 fully connected); last layer has 1000 neurons.
network_specifics.GoogLeNet: Introduced Inception module with parallel 1x1, 3x3, 5x5 convolution blocks and 3x3 max pooling blocks.
network_specifics.GoogLeNet-Places365: Variant of GoogLeNet pretrained on Places-365.

training_details.dataset: HAM10000 (10,015 images total; 3,501 training, 1,499 validation, 3,501 testing/weight establishment, 1,514 final testing).
training_details.data_augmentation: Shearing (vertical/horizontal), mirroring, random image zoom, vertical/horizontal pixel shift (max 10%).
training_details.class_balancing: Augmented underrepresented classes (melanoma, dermatofibroma, etc.) by adding 200 images each and removed corresponding images from the nevus class.

classification_or_output_head.type: Custom decision fusion module (Collective Decision Block).
classification_or_output_head.output: One of seven classes: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc.
classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Weighted voting based on confusion matrix performance on a dedicated testing set.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Shearing (vertical and horizontal axis), mirroring, random image zoom, and vertical/horizontal pixel shift (maximum 10%).
Class balancing: Augmentation of underrepresented classes (melanoma, dermatofibroma, etc.) by adding 200 images each and removal of corresponding images from the nevus class.
Image resizing: Resizing images to expected sizes for each network using an augmented image datastore.
Metadata parsing: Automatic reading of metadata files to group images into folders based on label/diagnosis.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Shearing and rotations on both vertical and horizontal axes; mirroring; vertical or horizontal pixel shift with a maximum of 10%.

Augmented underrepresented classes (melanoma, dermatofibroma, etc.) by adding 200 representative images each and removed the same number of images from the nevus class to balance training and validation datasets.

conclusions: The proposed Collective Intelligence-based System (CIS), which combines multiple convolutional neural networks via a decision fusion module, achieved a validation accuracy approximately 3 percent better than the best-performing individual network.
Networks based on residual connections (such as ResNet-50 and ResNet-101) and deeper architectures demonstrated higher accuracy, with performance improving as network depth increased.
The best-performing individual networks were ResNet-50 and DenseNet201, both achieving a validation accuracy of 93.5%.

The collective intelligence approach simulates a multi-specialist decision-making process, where weighted outputs from individual networks based on their specific performance lead to a more accurate final prediction than any single network alone.
Data augmentation and class balancing were necessary to address the imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, particularly for underrepresented classes like melanoma and dermatofibroma.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 1562 nevus samples vs. 179 dermatofibroma samples in training), potentially biasing the model towards more prevalent classes despite augmentation efforts.

The proposed Collective Intelligence System (CIS) uses a testing set (3501 images from the second part of HAM10000) to calculate the weight matrix for the ensemble; this constitutes a form of data leakage or optimistic bias because the ensemble weights are tuned on data that is part of the overall evaluation pool, rather than being derived solely from validation performance.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to support the claim that the ~3% improvement over the best individual network (ResNet-50/DenseNet-201) is statistically significant.
The implementation was executed on a PC without a GPU (single CPU only), which may limit the scalability and practical deployment speed of the multi-network ensemble in real-time clinical settings.

Several individual network architectures (ResNet-101, InceptionResNet-V2) reported extremely low validation accuracies (8% and 50.2% respectively), suggesting potential issues with hyperparameter tuning, convergence, or implementation errors for those specific models within the ensemble.

The system was evaluated only on the HAM10000 dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets (e.g., ISIC 2019/2020 or clinical cohorts), limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse populations or different dermoscopic equipment.

Data augmentation was applied specifically to balance classes by adding 200 images to underrepresented classes and removing corresponding images from the nevus class, which alters the natural class distribution and may not reflect real-world prevalence.
